coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric detection of hydrogen peroxide and lactate based on the etching of the carbon based Au-Ag bimetallic nanocomposite synthesized by carbon dots as the reductant and stabilizer
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lin
Hou, Wenli
Lu, Qiujun
Liu, Meiling
Chen, Chao
Zhang, Youyu
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: In this report, carbon-based gold core silver shell Au-Ag bimetallic nanocomposite (Au-Ag/C NC) was synthesized using carbon dots (C-dots) as the reductant and stabilizer by a facile green sequential reduction approach. The structure and morphology of the nanocomposite are characterized by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The as synthesized Au-Ag/C NC exhibits good optic response toward hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) without adding any other chromogenic agents. The characteristic surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorbance peak of Au-Ag/C NC declined and red-shifted with the solution color changing from reddish orange to light pink when adding H2O2 owing to the etching effect of H2O2 towards Ag. Thus, a simple colorimetric and UV strategy for sensitive detection of H2O2 is proposed. It provides the wide linear range for detection of H2O2 from 0.8–90 μM and 90–500 μM, and the detection limit was as low as 0.3 μM (S/N = 3). In addition, this colorimetric strategy can also be applied to directly distinguish and detect of lactate by naked eye and UV–Vis. The linear range of colorimetric sensing towards lactate was 0.1–22 μM and 22–220 μM, which was successfully applied in the analysis of lactate in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
Carbon-based Au-Ag bimetallic nanocomposite
H2O2
Lactate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric assay of H2O2 and l-lactate
Detection of l-lactate in serum samples
Detection of H2O2 and l-lactate based on Au-Ag/C NC
The response of C-dots, AuNPs/C NC, Au-Ag/C NC and AgNPs/C NC in the presence of H2O2
Detection of H2O2 based on colorimetric and UV spectra
Detection of l-lactate based on Au-Ag/C NC
Detection of l-lactate in serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21305042 , 21375037 , 21405042 ), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department ( 14B116 ), the Science and Technology Department ( 14JJ4030 ), the Opening Fund of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University ( 2013017 ), Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China) , Hunan Normal University ( KLCBTCMR2011-08 ) and key laboratory of Jiangxi Province FO Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle ( ST201422007 ), Collaborative Innovation Center of New Chemical Technologies for Environmental Benignity and Efficient Resource Utilization and the Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of C-dots (A), AuNPs/C NC (C) and Au-Ag/C NC (D). Size distribution histograms of C-dots (B).
(A) XPS survey spectrum of Au-Ag/C NC, high resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1s, (C) Au 4f and (D) Ag 3d in the Au-Ag/C NC.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-dots (a), AuNPs/C NC (b) and Au-Ag/C NC (c). (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra and photographs (inset) of as-synthesized AuNPs/C NC (a), AgNPs/C NC (h) and Au-Ag/C NC with different mass ratio of AgNO3 and HAuCl4 (b-g represents 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 7.5, 10.).
UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-dots (A), AuNPs/C NC (B), Au-Ag/C NC (C) and AgNPs/C NC (D) in the presence and absence of H2O2.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of Au-Ag/C NC treated without and with different concentrations of H2O2 from 0.8 μM to 500 μM. (B) Two linear calibration curves by plotting the absorbance change versus different concentrations of H2O2 within 0.8–90 μM and 90–500 μM.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of Au-Ag/C NC treated with different concentrations of l-lactate from 0.1 μM to 220 μM in the presence of LOx. (B) Two linear calibration curves by plotting the absorbance change versus different concentrations of l-lactate within 0.1–22 μM and 22–220 μM.
Illustration of the preparation of Au-Ag/C NC, and the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and l-lactate based on Au-Ag/C NC.
Comparison of several previously reported H2O2 sensors based on electrochemical, colorimetric and fluorimetric methods.
Comparison of several previously reported l-lactate sensors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Gold nanoparticles anchored on reduced graphene oxide and l-lactate dehydrogenase.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of l-lactate in serum samples.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric detection of hydrogen peroxide and lactate based on the etching of the carbon based Au-Ag bimetallic nanocomposite synthesized by carbon dots as the reductant and stabilizer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally (Lin Zhang and Wenli Hou).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this report, carbon-based gold core silver shell Au-Ag bimetallic nanocomposite (Au-Ag/C NC) was synthesized using carbon dots (C-dots) as the reductant and stabilizer by a facile green sequential reduction approach. The structure and morphology of the nanocomposite are characterized by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The as synthesized Au-Ag/C NC exhibits good optic response toward hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) without adding any other chromogenic agents. The characteristic surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorbance peak of Au-Ag/C NC declined and red-shifted with the solution color changing from reddish orange to light pink when adding H2O2 owing to the etching effect of H2O2 towards Ag. Thus, a simple colorimetric and UV strategy for sensitive detection of H2O2 is proposed. It provides the wide linear range for detection of H2O2 from 0.8–90 μM and 90–500 μM, and the detection limit was as low as 0.3 μM (S/N = 3). In addition, this colorimetric strategy can also be applied to directly distinguish and detect of lactate by naked eye and UV–Vis. The linear range of colorimetric sensing towards lactate was 0.1–22 μM and 22–220 μM, which was successfully applied in the analysis of lactate in human serum.

ce:section.ce:para: Bimetallic nanomaterials exhibit superior optical [1], magnetic [2], electronic [3], and catalytic [4] properties and have been widely applied in a variety of fields such as catalysts [4], fuel cell fabrication [5] and antibacterial activity [6], and the usage of bimetallic and multi-metallic system are important as they provide a convenient means to trim down the total amount of the expensive metals via dilution with commonly available metals and make full use of the advantage of both metals simultaneously. For example, Croy et al. reported the decomposition of methanol over a series of bimetallic Pt-M (M = Au, Pd, Ru, Fe) and analyzed the effect of the secondary metal on the oxidation state, activity and selectivity of Pt [7]. Han et al. prepared Au-Ag heterogeneous nanorods as nanozyme interfaces with peroxidase-like activity and applied them to detect of glucose at nearly neutral pH [8]. Kang et al. founded a highly sensitive colorimetric detection of glucose in serum based on the DNA-embedded Au-Ag core-shell nanoparticle [9].

It is known that the structure, morphology and properties of the bimetallic nanoparticles can be adjusted by using different reductants, stabilizers and surfactants. There are many methods for preparation of bimetallic materials. Normally, in the one-step reduction process, sodium borohydride [10], ascorbic acid [10,11], and formaldehyde [12] were used as the reductant in synthesis of bimetallic nanoparticles. In the sequential reduction method, two different reducing agents were usually used, which was complicated and would easily introduce some by-products [13]. Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) is the alternative reductant for preparation of bimetallic materials, which considered as a new carbon nanomaterial and attracted extensive attention in recent years. The good fluorescence property, low toxicity, good solubility and biocompatibility of C-dots [14,15] make them widely applied in the field of biosensors, catalysis, bioimaging and biomedical applications based on fluorescent method [14,16–22]. Moreover, the unique properties of C-dots were of importance since C-dots can act as both reductants and stabilizers in the preparation of various kinds of metallic nanoparticles without adding other extra reducing or protective reagents. For example, Zheng et al. synthesized C-dots by acidic oxidation method and then reduced the carbonyl and epoxy groups on the surface of C-dots to hydroxyl groups with NaBH4. The as prepared hydroxyl-rich C-dots were used to reduce HAuCl4 at 60 °C for 12 h to synthesize AuNPs [23]. Recently, our group constructed a model system to synthesize metallic nanoparticles in which C-dots acted as the reductant and stabilizer though a simple green procedure [19]. A facial and green synthesis method for carbon based AuPd bimetallic nanoparticles with the C-dots as the stabilizing agent and mild reducing agent through a sequential reduction strategy was proposed by us [13]. It is important to expand the application of C-dots owing to its special properties such as low toxicity and good biocompatibility and reducing property.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is of great importance in many fields, including bioanalysis for series of multi-parametric blood sample analysis (glucose, cholesterol, l-lactate and xanthine) [24–26], environmental protection [27], and chemical and biochemical industries [28]. Therefore, it is practically significant to monitor the concentration of H2O2 and some related biomolecules in both atmospheric science and biological science. So far, lots of methods have been developed to detect H2O2, such as colorimetry [8,29], electro analysis [30–32], fluorimetry [33,34] and so on, most of which are based on natural enzymes (especially peroxidase) [35,36]. At the same time, many colorimetric methods for H2O2 have been developed recently based on the peroxidase-like nanomaterials, which rely on the usage of chromogenic substances like 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) [11,13,19] and 2,2′-azino- bis(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) [8]. Therefore, some simple, green and low cost methods for non-enzyme detection of H2O2 and related molecules are also of great importance.

In this work, carbon-based Au-Ag bimetallic nanocomposite (Au-Ag/C NC) was synthesized via a facile green sequential reduction procedure using C-dots as both reductant and stabilizing agent and a simple and sensitive approach for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and l-lactate was proposed, as shown in Scheme 1 . The surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorbance of Au-Ag/C NC changed with obvious color altering when adding different concentration of H2O2. Thus, a simple colorimetric and UV strategy was proposed for sensitive detection of H2O2 and l-lactate owing to the etching of Ag on Au-Ag/C NC by H2O2. Benefiting from these outstanding optical and color change, wide linear range with high sensitivity and low detection limit was obtained. This method is green, facial and free of using any additional chromogenic substances, which makes it a potential and universal strategy for detection. It is believed that this work based on the etching will be widely used in analytical chemistry and biosensing areas.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, EG), platinum sheet (Pt), concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), silver nitrate (AgNO3), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium l-lactate (C3H5NaO3, optical purity) and lactate oxidase (LOx) were purchased from Sigma. Britton-Robinson buffer (BR buffer) was prepared using 0.04 M H3BO3, 0.04 M H3PO4 and 0.04 M CH3COOH and the pH values were adjusted by 0.2 M NaOH. All chemicals were of analytical grade. Milli-Q ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used in all experiments.

The electrolysis of C-dots was synthesized on the DC POWER SUPPLY PS-305DM. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were collected on a JEM-1230 transmission electronic microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on K-Alpha 1063 (Thermo Fisher Scientiﬁc, England). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, England) was used to characterize the size distribution of nanomaterials. Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) was recorded on the UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier Transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were collected on a pressed pellet with KBr in the transmission mode on a Nexus-670 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet Instrument Co. USA). The pH values were measured on a pH S-3C digital acidometer (PR China).

The hydroxyl-rich C-dots were synthesized by one-pot electrochemical carbonization of ethylene glycol [19] as can be seen in the detailed procedures in the supporting information. Au-Ag/C NC was synthesized by HAuCl4 and AgNO3 using hydroxyl-rich C-dots as the reductant and stabilizer, which was prepared via adding 20 μL HAuCl4 aqueous solution (5 mg mL −1) into 1 mL of the C-dots solution by simply mixing and reaction at 30 °C for 3 h. Taking advantage of the as-synthesized AuNPs/C NC as seed, a certain volume of AgNO3 (5 mg mL−1) and 0.01 M NaOH were added in, followed by reaction and incubation at 30 °C for over 12 h. The reddish orange Au-Ag/C NC solution was obtained and stored at 4 °C for further use.
For comparison, carbon-based Ag nanocomposite (AgNPs/C NC) was prepared by mixing 20 μL AgNO3 aqueous solution (5 mg mL−1), 500 μL NaOH (0.01 M) and 1 mL of the C-dots solution at 30 °C for 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric assay of H2O2 and l-lactate
ce:section.ce:para:
The detection of H2O2 was performed as follows: 200 μL of the as-synthesized Au-Ag/C NC solution, 100 μL BR buffer (0.04 M) and 300 μL H2O2 with different concentrations were added sequentially into 1.5 mL tubes and then incubated for a certain time. The photographs (against white light) and UV–Vis absorbance spectra in the range of 300–700 nm were recorded. A
0 and A represented the maximal absorbance of Au-Ag/C NC in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively.

For the detection of l-lactate, a certain volume of sodium l-lactate solution was firstly mixed with 10 μL of 1 mg/mL LOx in a 1.5 mL centrifugal tube and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. The analysis process was similar to that of the detection of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of l-lactate in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of l-lactate in serum samples was as follows: Human serum samples were provided by Hunan Normal University Hospital (Changsha, China). Serum samples were first treated by ultrafiltration with 5 kDa in the amicon cell at 13000 rpm for 20 min to remove the precipitation, and then the supernatant was transferred into calibrated test tubes. Serum samples were diluted 20 times using BR (0.04 M, pH 7.5) before measurement. 200 μL diluted serum samples with 100 μL l-lactate with different concentrations were firstly mixed with 10 μL 1 mg/mL LOx and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. The following steps are similar to that of detection of l-lactate.

Fig. 1 shows the morphology and microstructure of C-dots with the average diameter of 2.4 nm. The HRTEM image of single C-dots shows a crystalline structure with the lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, which can be attributed to the (102) diffraction planes of graphitic (sp2) carbon [19]. The AuNPs/C NC (Fig. 1C) showed good dispersion with the average diameter of 16 nm and the clear lattice spacing of 0.24 nm on the HRTEM image which was attributed to the (111) lattice space of metallic Au [19]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 1D, it could be clearly seen that the average size of Au-Ag/C NC is about 20 nm, which is obviously larger than the AuNPs/C NC and the HRTEM image of one Au-Ag/C nanocomposite shows a spherical-like structure with the gold core and silver shell, surrounded by some carbon dots. Therefore, after addition of AuCl4 −, AuCl4 − was reduced to AuNPs by C-dots, and the remaining C-dots may attach to the AuNPs surface through hydrogen bonding and used as the stabilizer to prevent the aggregation of AuNPs.

To further investigate the mechanism for the formation of nanoparticle in the presence of C-dots, FT-IR spectra of C-dots and AuNPs/C NC were conducted, and the change of the surface functional groups of C-dots can be characterized, as shown in Fig. S1 in the supporting information. The obvious peak at 3428 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 are assigned to the characteristic peak of hydroxyl groups (-OH) and carbonyl groups (CO), respectively, demonstrating the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the as-synthesized C-dots. After reacting with HAuCl4, the relative intensity of hydroxyl groups (3426 cm−1) on AuNPs/C NC decreased significantly and the intensity of carbonyl groups (1700 cm−1) increased, indicating that hydroxyl groups on the surface of C-dots may reduce HAuCl4 into AuNPs and convert themselves into carbonyl groups [37]. Therefore, in this report, C-dots acted as the reductant as well as the stabilizing agent in the synthesis of AuNPs and Au-Ag bimetallic nanocomposite.

XPS measurements were performed to determine the composition and chemical state of the as-prepared Au-Ag/C NC. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2 A) reveals the existence of Ag, Au, C and O elements. The C 1s (Fig. 2B) shows three distinct peaks at 284.9, 286.7 and 288.6 eV. The first peak can be ascribed to the carbon species adsorbed on surface of Au-Ag bimetallic nanocomposite [38], the second and the third binding energy peaks belong to the oxygen containing groups of C-O and CO groups [39], respectively. As shown in Fig. 2C, the Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 peaks in the spectrum of Au-Ag/C NC located at 84.8 and 88.2 eV [38,39], respectively, suggesting the formation of metallic Au (0) in the nanocomposite [39]. No obvious peak for Au3+ could be found, which also confirms the complete reduction of Au3+ to Au (0) in our designed process. The Ag 3d3/2 peak at 367.9 eV and Ag 3d5/2 peak at 373.9 eV (Fig. 2D) show that the slitting of the 3d doublet of Ag was 6.0 eV, which agreed well with the theoretic value of the metallic silver [29], demonstrating the chemical states of Ag on the surface of Au-Ag/C NC.

Fig. 3 A shows the UV spectra of C-dots, AuNPs/C NC and Au-Ag/C NC. It was found that C-dots display a strong absorption peak at 257 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π∗ transition [19]. AuNPs/C NC exhibited an absorption peak at 530 nm, matching well with the typical surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption peak of AuNPs, which indicated the successful formation of AuNPs. While for the Au-Ag/C NC, an absorption peak at 402 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum was observed when adding AgNO3 into the AuNPs/C NC solution with certain time of incubation. And this is the characteristic absorption band for AgNPs induced by the SPR absorption, which confirmed the formation of Au-Ag/C NC.

To investigate the effect of the concentration of HAuCl4 and AgNO3 on the SPR absorption, the ratio of AgNO3 to HAuCl4 was monitored. When the ratio of AgNO3 increased at the constant concentration of HAuCl4 for formation AuNPs/C, the absorption band of AuNPs decreased gradually, and another absorbance band around 410 nm was found as shown in Fig. 3B. Moreover, when the mass ratio of AgNO3 and HAuCl4 reached 7.5, only an obvious plasmon resonance band of AgNPs at 402 nm was observed. These above phenomena may suggest that Ag+ could be reduced into AgNPs by C-dots in the AuNPs/C NC solution and at the same time, the AgNPs may cover on the surface of AuNPs as a result. This can be confirmed by the TEM structure of Au-Ag/C NC. However, further increase of the concentration of AgNO3, the peak at 402 nm decreased, which means that the growth of AgNPs was probably hampered at higher concentration of AgNO3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 and l-lactate based on Au-Ag/C NC
The response of C-dots, AuNPs/C NC, Au-Ag/C NC and AgNPs/C NC in the presence of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The variation of UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-dots, AuNPs/C NC, Au-Ag/C NC and AgNPs/C NC in the absence and presence of H2O2 were shown in Fig. 4 . The UV–Vis spectra of C-dots and AuNPs/C NC altered scarcely after the addition of H2O2, which illustrated that there were no response between both C-dots and AuNPs/C NC with H2O2. On the contrary, Au-Ag/C NC and AgNPs/C NC exhibited large optic response with the addition of H2O2 and obvious color change can be observed. Comparing with the color fading of AgNPs/C NC ranging from yellow to light yellow, the color of Au-Ag/C NC solution transformed from orange to pink with the increase of concentrations of H2O2, which was more sensitive to be concerned by naked eye [9]. At the same time, Au-Ag/C NC-based method can obtain wider detection range, as can be seen in Fig. 4C–D. Moreover, compared with previously reported colorimetric sensors based on silver nanomaterials [29,40], two phenomena including the decline of SPR peak intensity and red-shift of the absorbance were observed which was superior to the single absorbance fading of AgNPs. Moreover, the color transition change was also sensitive to be observed. Therefore, we supposed that Au-Ag/C NC could be more suitable as the nanosensor to detect H2O2.

As shown in Fig. 4C, the characteristic band for Au-Ag/C NC changed dramatically in the absence and presence of different concentrations of H2O2. With the increase in concentration of H2O2, the SPR absorption intensity of Au-Ag/C NC decreased and the band red shifted. It was found that the average size of Au-Ag/C NC obviously decreased from 25.6 nm to 15.4 nm in the DLS experiment, as shown in Fig. S2 in the supporting information, which was probably attributed to the oxidation and etching of the silver on Au-Ag/C NC by H2O2 through the reaction as follows [40,41]:
A
g 0
+
H 2
O 2
→ A
g +
+
O 2
· −
+
H 2
O

With the increase of H2O2, the Ag shell on the Au-Ag/C NC was gradually etched and uncovered of the inner gold core gradually. This observation provides great potential for quantitative detection of H2O2.

In order to achieve the best performance for H2O2 detection, the effects such as reaction temperature, pH and reaction time in this system were investigated, and the results were shown clearly in Fig. S3 in the supporting information. The absorption variation reached its maximum at 35 °C and at pH = 7.5, respectively. And the absorbance changed sharply at the initial and reached the stable state until approximately 120 min. Herein, we chose 35 °C, pH = 7.5 and 120 min for reaction as the optimal reaction conditions in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 based on colorimetric and UV spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of H2O2 was performed under the optimized condition based on Au-Ag/C NC using the colorimetric and UV strategy, in which the concentrations of H2O2 increased. It was found that the color variation of the system was dependent on the H2O2 concentration. The lowest concentration of H2O2 which could be observed by naked eye was 10 μM. For further quantitative detection of H2O2, the UV absorbance spectra were monitored in the presence of different concentrations of H2O2. It was found that the maximum absorbance intensity of the band for the system decreased with the increase of concentrations of H2O2 from 0 to 500 μM (Fig. 5 A). As shown in Fig. 5B, the maximum absorbance was proportional to H2O2 concentration, providing a wide linear detection range from 0.8 to 90 μM and 90–500 μM with a limit of detection of 0.3 μM (S/N = 3) and the regression equation were A = 0.0034[H2O2]+0.0394 (R = 0.997) and A = 0.0004[H2O2]+0.315 (R = 0.998), respectively. According to Fig. 5, there is still obvious absorbance around 420 nm, which is related to the characteristic surface plasmon resonance absorbance peak of AgNPs. Therefore, the Ag layer wasn't be totally etched by H2O2. Comparison of several previously reported H2O2 detection by electrochemical, colorimetric and fluorimetric methods with this proposed colorimetric method is shown in Table 1 . It indicate that this method is simple, green, facile, and provides a wide linear range and a low detect limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of l-lactate based on Au-Ag/C NC
ce:section.ce:para:
l-lactate can be specifically oxidized by LOx in the presence of oxygen to form H2O2 and the reaction is described as follows [42]:
L - lactate +
O 2
+
H 2
O → pyruvate +
H 2
O 2

Thus, combining this reaction with the as-prepared Au-Ag/C NC, highly sensitive detection of l-lactate can be constructed based on the color changes of Au-Ag/C NC associating with the produced H2O2 for etching Ag. The concentration change of l-lactate which could be observed by the naked eye, and the lowest discrimination concentration was 1 μM. Two linear calibration curves were obtained by plotting the decrease of the absorbance of Au-Ag/C NC versus l-lactate concentration in 0.1–22 μM and 22–220 μM, as shown in Fig. 6 . The colorimetric strategy based on Au-Ag/C NC for l-lactate detection was seldom reported and it was simple and green, free of using any additional colorant or chromogenic substances. Moreover, as shown in Table 2 , comparing with other electrochemical and fluorimetric methods for l-lactate detection, this system is much more sensitive with lower detection limit of 0.03 μM (S/N = 3).

The effect of some potential interfering species on the signal change of the proposed l-lactate biosensor was evaluated by exposing the biosensor into the 1 mM interferants in BR buffer solution at pH 7.5. It is found that no current signal was observed by injecting ascorbic acid, uric acid, paracetamol, l-cysteine, and glucose in solution for the l-lactate biosensor. It is noteworthy that bovine serum albumin and casein, which often cause interference on real food sample analysis, did not have any effect on the proposed l-lactate biosensor. Moreover, many substances such as some ions and cations often found in dairy products and serum samples do not exhibit significant interference on the determination of l-lactate.

The synthesized Au-Ag/C NC solution can monitor the l-lactate contents in aqueous solutions for at least one month. The precision of the biosensor was evaluated at 0.10 mM l-lactate and the relative standard deviation (n = 10) was less than 5.0%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of l-lactate in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of l-lactate in serum samples was detected by the Au-Ag/C NC combing with the LOx. Taking into consideration of the normal l-lactate level in healthy human blood as well as the linear range of our method, standard addition experiment was used to evaluate the new sensing platform. Different concentrations (0, 100, 2000 μM) of l-lactate were added into the sample and then diluted with buffer and the results were listed in Table 3 . The recoveries ranged from 93.1% to 104.0% with the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) less than 4.51%. These results indicated that the proposed Au-Ag/C NC sensing platform was capable of detecting l-lactate sensitively in real serum samples.

In summary, a facile and green preparation strategy for Au-Ag/C NC was successfully developed and sensitive nanosensor for H2O2 and l-lactate were proposed by the gradually etching of Ag on Au-Ag/C NC by H2O2. This can be observed by the SPR absorbance declining and red-shift of Au-Ag/C NC with the solution color changing from reddish orange to light pink, which can be recognized easily by the naked eye. Thus, the simple colorimetric and UV strategy proposed here may become a universal way for detection of H2O2 and some related biomolecules in both atmospheric science and biological science.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305042, 21375037, 21405042), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (14B116), the Science and Technology Department (14JJ4030), the Opening Fund of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University (2013017), Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), Hunan Normal University (KLCBTCMR2011-08) and key laboratory of Jiangxi Province FO Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle (ST201422007), Collaborative Innovation Center of New Chemical Technologies for Environmental Benignity and Efficient Resource Utilization and the Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.10.011.
